topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered as I pulled the heavy metal door open. My heart gave a sudden, unwelcome lurch against my ribs. There, nestled amongst the junk flyers and utility bills, was a card. It was thick, cream-colored, and entirely blank save for a single, elegant script at the top: "Happy Birthday." My fingers trembled as I picked it up. It felt oddly cold, almost clammy. I turned it over, searching for a signature, a name, anything to anchor it in reality. There was nothing. Just the message, stark and silent. A prickling sensation started at the base of my neck, and I instinctively dropped the card, snatching it up again, my breath catching in my throat. I backed away from the mailbox, my gaze darting to the street, half-expecting someone to be watching.

[story 2]

I hadn't expected anything. Just the usual deluge of paper, but this one felt different. It was a card, slipped into the slot, completely unaddressed. When I retrieved it, the paper seemed unnaturally smooth under my fingertips. My palms grew instantly damp. I unfolded it slowly, my eyes scanning the surface, searching for any telltale mark—a smudge, a stray ink mark. There was nothing. Only the looping, perfectly formed greeting. A strange tightness constricted my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I stood frozen by the front door, listening intently. Every distant sound—a car horn, the rustle of leaves—seemed amplified, sharp and intrusive. I needed to throw it away, to discard the anomaly, but my hand remained hovering over the paper, paralyzed by an internal tremor.

[story 3]

It was a Tuesday afternoon, and I was retrieving the mail when I saw it. A card. Unsigned. It was sitting right on top of the stack, stark white against the dull brown envelopes. As I lifted it, a wave of intense unease washed over me, making my vision swim slightly at the edges. I held it close to my face, trying to discern any hidden detail, any clue to its origin. The silence in my small living room seemed to deepen, pressing in on me. My jaw tightened, and I could feel a distinct vibration starting deep in my core. I dropped the card onto the worn rug as if it were suddenly hot, scrambling backward until my back hit the cool plaster of the hallway wall. I couldn't bring myself to look at it again.
